questions.question: Define a pure virtual function and explain its syntax.
keyPoints: Pure virtual function is declared using 'virtual' keyword with '= 0' syntax
It has no implementation in the base class
Syntax: virtual return_type function_name() = 0;
Purpose is to create an interface that derived classes must implement

questions.question: What is an abstract class? Can it be instantiated? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Abstract class contains at least one pure virtual function
Cannot be instantiated directly
Used as base class for derived classes
Provides interface and may contain implemented functions

questions.question: Explain the need for virtual destructor in abstract classes.
keyPoints: To ensure proper cleanup when deleting through base pointer
Without virtual destructor, only base destructor gets called
This leads to memory leaks and undefined behavior
Derived class destructor must be called for complete cleanup

questions.question: Differentiate between early binding and late binding with examples.
keyPoints: Early binding: function calls resolved at compile time (e.g., normal function calls, overloaded functions)
Late binding: function calls resolved at runtime (e.g., virtual functions)
Virtual functions use vtable for dynamic dispatch
Example: Calling virtual function through base pointer exhibits late binding

questions.question: Can abstract classes have constructors? Explain with reason.
keyPoints: Yes, abstract classes can have constructors
Constructors are needed to initialize base class members
Derived class constructors call abstract class constructor
Even though abstract class cannot be instantiated, its constructor is used during derived object creation

questions.question: What is the purpose of vtable in C++? How does it enable polymorphism?
keyPoints: vtable (virtual table) is a lookup table created by compiler
Each class with virtual functions has its own vtable
Contains pointers to virtual function implementations
At runtime, correct function is called through vtable based on actual object type

questions.question: Explain the diamond problem in multiple inheritance and how virtual inheritance solves it.
keyPoints: Diamond problem: ambiguity when class inherits from two classes sharing a common base
Leads to duplicate inheritance of base class members
Virtual inheritance: use 'virtual' keyword in base class specification
Ensures only one copy of base class members is inherited

questions.question: Design an abstract class 'Shape' with pure virtual function 'area()' and 'perimeter()'. Create two derived classes 'Circle' and 'Rectangle' that implement these functions. Write a main function demonstrating polymorphism.
keyPoints: Define abstract class Shape with pure virtual functions
Circle class with radius, implements area and perimeter
Rectangle class with length and width, implements area and perimeter
Use base class pointer to demonstrate runtime polymorphism
Show object slicing prevention using pointers
Proper virtual destructor in base class

questions.question: Explain the concept of interfaces in C++ with an example. How do interfaces help in achieving abstraction and polymorphism?
keyPoints: Interface: abstract class with only pure virtual functions
Example: interface with pure virtual functions for operations
Interfaces define contracts without implementation
Achieve abstraction by hiding implementation details
Enable polymorphism through different implementations
Support loosely coupled design and dependency inversion

questions.question: What is object slicing? How does it occur and how can it be prevented? Explain with a code example.
keyPoints: Object slicing: derived object assigned to base object loses derived portion
Occurs when object is passed by value to base class parameter
Prevents polymorphism as derived-specific members are lost
Prevention: use pointers or references
Alternative: use smart pointers for automatic memory management
Code example showing both scenarios

questions.question: Explain the Factory Design Pattern implementation using pure virtual functions. Write a complete C++ program demonstrating this pattern.
keyPoints: Factory Pattern: creational pattern using abstract base class
Define abstract Product class with pure virtual create method
Concrete Product classes implement the interface
Factory class creates objects without specifying exact class
Demonstrates loose coupling and extensibility
Shows how pure virtual functions enable object creation through interface
